title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe (Mole Negro)
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of traditional Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with our authentic recipe. Rich, complex, and deeply flavorful – perfect for your next Mexican dinner.
longTailKeywords: how to make Oaxacan Black Mole
Mole Negro Oaxacan step by step
best Oaxacan Black Mole at home
seo.h1: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Oaxacan Black Mole
Expert Tips for the Best Mole Negro
Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro Oaxacan
Variations and Substitutions for Black Mole
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of rich, dark Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with chicken and rice.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece.

description: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary masterpiece from Oaxaca, Mexico, known for its deep, complex flavors. This traditional sauce features a rich blend of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and a hint of chocolate, creating an unforgettable savory and subtly sweet experience. It is often served with chicken or turkey, transforming any meal into a festive occasion.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the deseeded chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for about 30 seconds per side until aromatic. Be careful not to burn them. Transfer the toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft. Drain and set aside.

instructions.title: Roast Aromatics, Nuts, and Seeds
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, pecans, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside. Next, lightly char the tomatillos, tomatoes, onion, and garlic in the skillet until softened. Set aside.

instructions.title: Fry Remaining Ingredients
instructions.text: Heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil in a pan. Fry the raisins, plantain slices, and torn tortillas until lightly golden. Be careful with the raisins as they can puff up quickly. Remove and drain any excess oil. Combine all roasted and fried ingredients with the rehydrated chiles.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted vegetables, fried ingredients, toasted nuts and seeds, cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin seeds, oregano, and thyme. Add about 1 cup of hot chicken broth. Blend until a very smooth paste forms, adding more broth as needed to facilitate blending. This may require blending in batches.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 2 tablespoons of lard or oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot fat. Cook, stirring constantly, for at least 20-30 minutes, allowing the mole to darken and thicken. This crucial step deepens the flavor. Gradually add the remaining chicken broth, stirring constantly to prevent lumps, until you reach your desired consistency. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Stir in the Mexican chocolate and sugar until melted and fully incorporated. Season with salt to taste. The mole should have a balanced sweet, savory, and spicy flavor profile. Simmer for another 10-15 minutes to allow the flavors to meld. Serve hot over cooked chicken or turkey, accompanied by rice and warm tortillas. Garnish with a sprinkle of sesame seeds.

tips: Toasting chiles and spices properly is key to unlocking their full flavor. Watch them closely to prevent burning, which can make the mole bitter.
For an even smoother mole, pass the blended sauce through a fine-mesh sieve or a food mill after blending. This removes any lingering bits of chile skin or seeds.
Mole Negro improves with age. Consider making it a day ahead and gently reheating it. It also freezes well for up to 3 months.
article:
Dive into the heart of Mexican culinary tradition with one of its most revered and complex dishes: Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro. Far more than just a sauce, this iconic creation from the southwestern state of Oaxaca is a symphony of flavors, a testament to patience, and a celebration of indigenous ingredients. If you're looking to elevate your Mexican dinner recipes repertoire, mastering this profound and delicious mole is an unforgettable journey. Prepare to embark on a culinary adventure that will transform your kitchen into a hub of rich aromas and deep, satisfying tastes.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

Oaxacan Black Mole, often hailed as the "king of moles," is a dark, incredibly rich, and complex sauce known for its intricate balance of sweet, smoky, spicy, and savory notes. Unlike other moles that might lean towards red or green hues, Mole Negro achieves its distinctive deep black color from a meticulous process of charring and toasting a variety of chiles and other ingredients. Originating from Oaxaca, a region famous for its "seven moles," Mole Negro stands out as the most elaborate and time-consuming, often reserved for special occasions like weddings, fiestas, and important family gatherings. It’s a dish that embodies the soul of Oaxacan gastronomy, a culinary masterpiece passed down through generations. The depth of flavor comes from a lengthy list of ingredients, typically including 6-7 different types of chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, and even a touch of chocolate, all blended and simmered to perfection.

## Ingredients for Authentic Mole Negro

Creating an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole is an investment in ingredients, each playing a crucial role in the final flavor profile. The exact proportions can vary by family recipe, but these are the core components:

The Chiles: This is the backbone of Mole Negro. You'll need a variety, typically including:
*   **Chile Mulato:** Provides a deep, earthy, and slightly smoky flavor.
*   **Chile Pasilla Mexicana:** Adds a dark, fruity, and mildly spicy note.
*   **Chile Ancho:** Contributes a sweet, raisin-like flavor and mild heat.
*   **Chile Chilhuacle Negro:** (If available) A rare and highly prized Oaxacan chile, essential for true authenticity, offering unique smoky and complex notes. Often substituted with more mulato or pasilla.
*   **Chile Guajillo:** Bright, fruity, and moderately spicy.
*   **Chile Chipotle:** For a touch of smoky heat.
*   **Chile Tabaiche or other small, hot chiles:** For an additional kick.

Aromatics and Spices: These provide the savory and fragrant layers.
*   **Onion and Garlic:** Foundation of savory flavor.
*   **Tomatillos and Tomatoes:** Add acidity and body.
*   **Cinnamon Stick, Cloves, Black Peppercorns:** Classic warm spices.
*   **Mexican Oregano and Thyme:** Earthy herbal notes.
*   **Anise Seeds, Cumin Seeds:** Aromatic and distinctive.

Thickeners and Richness: These create the signature texture and depth.
*   **Nuts:** Almonds, peanuts, pecans – for richness and thickening.
*   **Seeds:** Sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds (pepitas) – for nutty flavor and texture.
*   **Tortillas or Bread:** Stale tortillas or a bolillo (Mexican roll) are toasted and used as a thickener.
*   **Plantains or Raisins:** For natural sweetness and body.

Sweeteners and Fats: Balancing the heat and creating a glossy finish.
*   **Mexican Chocolate (Tableta):** Unsweetened or semi-sweet, crucial for depth, not just sweetness.
*   **Piloncillo (Mexican unrefined brown sugar) or Brown Sugar:** To balance bitterness and acidity.
*   **Lard or Vegetable Oil:** For frying the mole paste to develop its flavors.
*   **Chicken or Turkey Broth:** To achieve the desired consistency.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Oaxacan Black Mole

Making Oaxacan Black Mole is a labor of love, but each step builds upon the last to create something truly magnificent.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully toast all the dried chiles on a comal or dry skillet until aromatic and slightly pliable, being careful not to burn them (which would make the mole bitter). Remove stems and seeds (reserving some seeds for charring if desired), then soak them in hot water until softened.
2.  **Char and Toast Other Ingredients:** On the same comal, char the tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, and garlic until softened and slightly blackened. Separately, toast the nuts, seeds, and spices until fragrant. Toast the stale tortillas or bread until crisp.
3.  **Blend in Stages:** Drain the softened chiles and blend them with a portion of chicken broth until very smooth. Strain this mixture through a fine-mesh sieve to remove any tough chile skins, ensuring a silky texture. Separately, blend the charred aromatics, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, and toasted bread/tortillas with more broth until smooth.
4.  **Cook the Mole Paste:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or cazuela, heat a generous amount of lard or oil over medium heat. Pour in the chile puree, stirring constantly to prevent sticking. Cook for 15-20 minutes, allowing it to reduce and deepen in color. Then, add the blended aromatic and thickening mixture. Continue to cook, stirring frequently, for at least 30-40 minutes, or even longer. This frying process is crucial for developing the deep, complex flavors.
5.  **Incorporate Sweeteners and Chocolate:** Stir in the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar) until fully melted and incorporated. Add more chicken broth gradually, stirring until the mole reaches your desired consistency – typically thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.
6.  **Simmer and Season:** Reduce the heat to low and let the Oaxacan Black Mole simmer gently for at least an hour, or even several hours, stirring occasionally. This allows the flavors to meld and deepen. Season with salt to taste, adjusting sweetness or acidity as needed. The longer it simmers, the more complex it becomes.

## Expert Tips for the Best Mole Negro

*   **Patience is Key:** This is not a quick dish. Allow ample time for toasting, blending, and especially simmering. The long cooking time is essential for the flavors to fully develop and integrate.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** This is perhaps the most critical step. Over-toasting chiles will result in a bitter mole. Toast them just until aromatic and slightly pliable.
*   **Strain, Strain, Strain:** For the silkiest Oaxacan Black Mole, straining the chile puree is non-negotiable. It removes any fibrous bits, ensuring a luxurious texture.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** As you simmer, continually taste your mole. It's a balance of many flavors. You might need a pinch more salt, a touch more piloncillo, or a splash of broth to achieve perfection.
*   **Resting Improves Flavor:** Like a fine wine, Oaxacan Black Mole often tastes even better the next day after the flavors have had more time to meld and deepen. Make it ahead of time if possible.
*   **Source Quality Ingredients:** The complexity of the mole relies on the quality of its components, especially the chiles. Seek out fresh, authentic Mexican ingredients from a reputable source.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro Oaxacan

Oaxacan Black Mole is traditionally served with poultry, most commonly chicken or turkey. The rich, dark sauce beautifully complements the mild flavor of white meat. Simply simmer cooked chicken pieces in the mole until heated through and coated. It can also be served with pork, especially tenderloin or shoulder.

*   **Classic Pairing:** Serve with warm corn tortillas for scooping up every last drop of the exquisite sauce.
*   **Accompaniments:** A simple side of fluffy white rice is perfect for soaking up the mole. A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds for garnish adds a lovely texture and nutty accent.
*   **Vegetarian Option:** While traditionally meat-based, Mole Negro can be served over roasted vegetables like sweet potatoes or cauliflower, or with cheese enchiladas for a vegetarian twist.

## Variations and Substitutions for Black Mole

While the traditional Oaxacan Black Mole recipe is sacred to many, home cooks often adapt it based on ingredient availability or personal preference.

*   **Chile Substitutions:** If Chilhuacle Negro chiles are unavailable, increase the amount of mulato and pasilla chiles to compensate for the depth of flavor. A small amount of very dark roasted bell pepper can also add depth.
*   **Nut Alternatives:** Any combination of almonds, peanuts, pecans, or even cashews can be used. If there are nut allergies, pumpkin seeds and sesame seeds can be increased, and toasted oats or rice can be used as additional thickeners.
*   **Chocolate Choice:** While Mexican chocolate is preferred, high-quality unsweetened or dark chocolate (70% cacao or higher) can be used, adjusting the piloncillo for sweetness.
*   **Vegetarian Broth:** For a vegetarian or vegan version, simply substitute chicken broth with a rich vegetable broth.
*   **Spice Adjustments:** Feel free to adjust the amount of spices to your taste. Some like a stronger cinnamon presence, others prefer more cumin.

Embarking on the creation of Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary journey that promises immense reward. It's a dish that tells a story of tradition, patience, and the incredible diversity of Mexican dinner recipes. While it demands time and dedication, the resulting depth of flavor and the joy of sharing such a magnificent meal make every effort worthwhile. This isn't just food; it's an experience, a taste of Oaxaca's soul.
